harvestedTerms.summary: OpenAI article explaining how 'goblin' metaphors spread through GPT-5.1 to GPT-5.5 via reinforcement learning feedback loops tied to the 'Nerdy' personality training signal. The piece covers reward signal leakage, SFT data contamination, and behavioral generalization across model generations.

article.title: AI evals are becoming the new compute bottleneck

harvestedTerms.summary: Article from Hugging Face Blog argues that AI evaluation has crossed a cost threshold making it a primary compute bottleneck, documenting specific dollar costs for agent leaderboards, static benchmarks, and training-in-the-loop scientific ML benchmarks. Key themes: eval cost scaling, scaffold sensitivity, benchmark compression techniques, and the asymmetry between training and evaluation compute.

article.title: Intel Earnings, Intel’s Differentiation?, Whither Terafab

harvestedTerms.summary: Article covers Intel's earnings driven by CPU demand for AI workloads and discusses 'Terafab', a manufacturing/infrastructure concept. The excerpt is paywalled so detail is limited, but key themes are AI-driven CPU demand shifts and Intel's strategic differentiation in AI infrastructure.

article.title: LWiAI Podcast #238 - GPT 5.4 mini, OpenAI Pivot, Mamba 3, Attention Residuals

harvestedTerms.summary: Podcast episode covering GPT-5.4 mini/nano release, Mistral Small 4 MoE model, agent OS competition with Manus/Nvidia NeMo, DLSS 5, OpenAI enterprise pivot, and safety research on steganography, chain-of-thought faithfulness, and emergent misalignment defenses.

article.title: New Paper: Towards a science of AI agent reliability

harvestedTerms.summary: Article presents a research paper decomposing AI agent reliability into 12 dimensions across four high-level categories (consistency, robustness, calibration/predictability, safety), finding that capability gains have outpaced reliability gains across major model providers over 18 months. Key concepts include the capability-reliability gap, multi-dimensional reliability measurement, and the idea that accuracy alone is insufficient for autonomous deployment.

article.title: We need RSS for sharing abundant vibe-coded apps

harvestedTerms.summary: Short post about vibe-coded apps becoming so frequent and personal that they need RSS/Atom feed infrastructure for discovery and sharing — analogous to blog posts. Minimal new terminology but reinforces vibe-coding and touches on micro-app publishing patterns.
